summary: Justin Brennan conducts a mentorship Q&A covering wealth protection/currency diversification (off-topic) and deal analysis fundamentals. The relevant portion focuses on Rose's Indianapolis multifamily deals ($3.8M, 30-unit property and others) where Justin teaches specific underwriting steps, financial documentation requirements, and valuation methodology for value-add multifamily deals.
takeaways: Require T12 and rent roll before writing LOI: 'Send me over the T12 and rent roll and I'll go ahead and analyze and get you an LOI based off the financials'
Use 50% expense ratio as baseline for underwriting Midwest 1960s properties: 'You're in a 1962 property in the Midwest, upper Midwest. You're going to be right around 50-55% expense ratio'
Interior renovation budgets: '7 to 15,000 a unit for interior renos, light to heavy. And then your exterior maybe five or so thousand exterior per unit'
Never get attached to asking price; let financials dictate offer: 'I never get attached to what they ask. I watch what the property's telling me'
Use Goal Seek in Excel to back into purchase price based on cap rate or IRR targets
Ask brokers for 3 vendor referrals: PM companies, local commercial lenders/CMBs, and renovation/construction contractors

Deferred maintenance items (roof replacement) warrant price reduction or seller credit, not capital raise: 'that's a deferred maintenance item that you'd probably be asking for all or portion of credit for from the seller'
Verify lease documents: sample audit to confirm rent roll names/terms match actual lease agreements

skill_candidates.change: Systematically require complete financials (T12 + rent roll) before underwriting, rather than proceeding with P&L alone or making LOI without rent detail
skill_candidates.evidence: Rose provided P&L but lacked rent roll; Justin mandates: 'You need to call this guy and you say, Listen, sir. I need the financials so I can underwrite this deal and give you a proper LOI on the financials. Send me over the rent roll, the T12'

skill_candidates.change: Apply 50-55% expense ratio assumption for older Midwest properties rather than relying on seller's stated expenses
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin walks through Rose's $3.8M Indianapolis 1962 property and states: 'You're in a 1962 property in the Midwest, upper Midwest. You're going to have snow. You're going to have this. You're going to have that. I guarantee you're going to be right around 50-55% expense ratio'

skill_candidates.change: Use Excel Goal Seek to back into rational purchase price from required IRR/cap rate, stopping when assumptions break down instead of negotiating from asking price
skill_candidates.evidence: When Rose's deal 'implodes' at $3.8M, Justin uses Goal Seek to show deal only makes sense at $1.621M: 'You're just backing into numbers. That's what I'm going to write...goal seek...set cell...by changing that'

skill_candidates.change: Request and document three vendor categories from each broker (PMs, lenders, contractors) to build local market team simultaneously while sourcing deals
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin tells Rose: 'I would talk to your broker friend that's super nice to you and say, Hey, do you have a couple other PM companies that you can refer me to...same question of the property manager. This is how you're going to get your little team in Indianapolis set up'

title: Tuesday Mentorship Q&A - Wealth Protection & Currency Shift _06-03-25 | Justin Brennan
